refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall enable end-to-end teleoperation communication with latency that permits safe remote operation.
refined_fr.rationale: The original FR conflates two concerns: (1) the functional requirement to enable teleoperation communication, and (2) a performance constraint on latency. The refined FR isolates the core functional obligation (enable teleoperation) while deferring the quantitative latency threshold to acceptance criteria and design constraints. This keeps the FR solution-neutral and problem-focused. The phrase 'compatible with safe remote operation' is vague and belongs in the constraint layer; the FR now states the functional intent without prescribing how safety is achieved.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Demonstrate that teleoperation communication is established end-to-end (operator input → remote equipment → feedback to operator) under nominal network conditions. Measure latency distribution and confirm p95 ≤ 150 ms per acceptance criteria. Validate that measured latency does not violate safety operational policies.
sub_frs: FR-NGHE-008.1: The NGHE solution shall transmit operator control commands from the tele-operator to the remote equipment.
FR-NGHE-008.2: The NGHE solution shall transmit equipment state and sensor feedback from the remote equipment to the tele-operator.
FR-NGHE-008.3: The NGHE solution shall maintain teleoperation communication continuity under agreed network conditions.

candidate_dps: DP1: Implement a deterministic, low-latency communication pipeline with end-to-end latency monitoring and adaptive quality-of-service (QoS) prioritization to ensure operator commands and sensor feedback remain within the p95 ≤ 150 ms budget under agreed network conditions.

DP2: Establish a dual-channel redundant communication architecture (primary + failover) with independent latency paths and automatic switchover logic to guarantee safe operation continuity when primary path degrades.

DP3: Deploy edge-local command buffering and predictive state synchronization at the remote site to decouple operator input latency from network round-trip time, reducing effective operator-perceived latency.
DP4: Implement a time-synchronized, event-driven messaging protocol with priority queuing and jitter compensation to enforce deterministic latency bounds across heterogeneous network conditions.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP1: Implement a deterministic, low-latency communication pipeline with end-to-end latency monitoring and adaptive quality-of-service (QoS) prioritization to ensure operator commands and sensor feedback remain within the p95 ≤ 150 ms budget under agreed network conditions.

potential_sources: DP1 assumes a single, unified communication pipeline; if operator control and sensor feedback use different physical paths or protocols, latency budgets may not compose linearly—recommend explicit separation of command and telemetry channels as sub-DPs.

QoS prioritization logic is tightly coupled to network stack behavior; if network conditions violate assumptions (e.g., sustained congestion), adaptive mechanisms may not recover within budget—mitigation: define fallback policies and safety interlocks.
Latency monitoring itself introduces measurement overhead; if instrumentation is not carefully isolated, it can inflate observed latency—recommend out-of-band or hardware-timestamped telemetry.
DP1 does not explicitly address operator safety during transient latency spikes; recommend pairing with operational policies (e.g., reduced speed during high-latency windows) to close the safety gap.
coupling_check.notes: DP1 is solution-neutral (no specific protocol or vendor named) and logically sound for the stated FR. It avoids premature technology lock-in while establishing clear latency and monitoring boundaries. Coupling is moderate: the design depends on network conditions being 'agreed' and stable; if conditions are unpredictable, DP2 (redundancy) or DP3 (edge buffering) may be necessary complements. DP1 alone is insufficient for safety; it must be paired with operational policies and failsafe mechanisms.

assumptions_risks: ASSUMPTION: 'Agreed network conditions' are well-defined, stable, and measurable. RISK: If network conditions are vague or change unexpectedly (e.g., interference, congestion), latency budget may be violated. MITIGATION: Establish explicit SLA with network provider; implement continuous monitoring and alerting.

ASSUMPTION: Operator and remote equipment are synchronized on time and state. RISK: Clock drift or state divergence can cause safety-critical misalignment (e.g., operator believes equipment is stopped, but it is still moving). MITIGATION: Implement heartbeat/watchdog logic and periodic state reconciliation.

ASSUMPTION: Latency budget of 150 ms is sufficient for safe remote operation under all worksite conditions. RISK: Operator reaction time + equipment response time may exceed 150 ms in edge cases (e.g., emergency stop). MITIGATION: Validate with human factors analysis and worksite safety plan; consider adding local safety interlocks (e.g., dead-man switch, geofencing).

ASSUMPTION: QoS prioritization can be enforced end-to-end without vendor lock-in. RISK: If network infrastructure does not support priority tagging or if ISP deprioritizes traffic, adaptive QoS may fail. MITIGATION: Negotiate QoS guarantees in network SLA; design fallback to lower-bandwidth, higher-latency mode.

ASSUMPTION: Monitoring and adaptive mechanisms do not introduce latency overhead that consumes the budget. RISK: Instrumentation, logging, and decision logic can add 10–50 ms. MITIGATION: Profile and optimize monitoring; use hardware timestamps and out-of-band telemetry.

ASSUMPTION: Worksite safety plan and operational policies are in place and enforced. RISK: If policies are absent or not followed, DP1 alone does not guarantee safe operation. MITIGATION: Ensure policies are documented, trained, and audited; integrate policy enforcement into system design (e.g., speed limits, geofencing).
selected_dp.recommended_revision: FR (refined): The NGHE solution shall enable end-to-end teleoperation communication with latency that permits safe remote operation, achieving p95 ≤ 150 ms under agreed network conditions, and shall include monitoring, failsafe mechanisms, and integration with worksite safety policies to ensure operator and equipment safety during transient latency spikes or network degradation. REVISED DP: Implement a deterministic, low-latency communication pipeline with (1) separate command and telemetry channels, (2) end-to-end latency monitoring with hardware timestamps, (3) adaptive QoS prioritization with explicit fallback policies, (4) periodic state reconciliation and watchdog logic to detect operator–equipment desynchronization, and (5) integration points for worksite safety interlocks (e.g., speed limits, geofencing, emergency stop) to ensure safe operation within and beyond the 150 ms latency budget. This revision makes explicit the safety-critical dependencies and operational policies that DP1 alone cannot address, improving traceability and reducing risk of unsafe operation due to latency spikes or network failures.

im_boundary.name: Teleoperation
im_boundary.scope: Encapsulates the deterministic communication pipeline, latency monitoring, and adaptive QoS prioritization logic that bridges operator commands and sensor feedback within the p95 ≤ 150 ms budget.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Receives operator commands and control directives; Teleoperation module enforces deterministic scheduling and QoS prioritization on ingress.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Transmits sensor feedback and latency telemetry; Teleoperation module monitors end-to-end latency and adaptive QoS state.

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Abstracts underlying network transport; Teleoperation module manages deterministic pipeline scheduling, latency measurement, and QoS adaptation at the link interface.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Signals safety-critical latency violations or QoS degradation; Teleoperation module detects and escalates breaches of p95 ≤ 150 ms budget to Safety Services.

module_containment_scores.notes: Excellent cohesion: deterministic pipeline, latency monitoring, and QoS prioritization are core teleoperation concerns. Minimal diffusion: SafetyAlert escalation is a clean outbound interface; Network Link abstraction isolates transport details. Ownership is natural—teleoperation owns end-to-end latency SLA.

Poor fit: Vehicle module typically owns actuators and sensor hardware, not communication pipeline logic. Latency monitoring and QoS prioritization would diffuse across Vehicle and Teleoperation, creating coupling. Violates separation of concerns.

Weak containment: Safety Services should consume latency alerts and enforce safety policies, not own the pipeline itself. Placing DP here couples safety logic to communication implementation, reducing modularity and testability.

Overly broad: NGHE Solution is a system-level container. Placing DP here lacks specificity and obscures ownership. Diffusion occurs because latency monitoring and QoS are teleoperation-specific concerns, not system-wide.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: The Teleoperation module is the natural owner of the deterministic communication pipeline, latency monitoring, and adaptive QoS prioritization because: (1) these mechanisms directly enable operator command delivery and sensor feedback within agreed latency budgets; (2) teleoperation is the functional domain where end-to-end latency SLA is defined and measured; (3) the module boundary cleanly separates communication logic (Teleoperation) from safety policy enforcement (Safety Services) and vehicle actuation (Vehicle); (4) external interfaces (Tasking, Telemetry, Network Link, SafetyAlert) align naturally with teleoperation responsibilities.
containment_risks: Network Link abstraction must be sufficiently detailed to allow Teleoperation to measure and adapt QoS without leaking transport-layer specifics; if Network Link is opaque, latency monitoring may require intrusive coupling.
SafetyAlert escalation must be non-blocking and deterministic; if Safety Services responds synchronously, it could violate the p95 ≤ 150 ms budget. Recommend asynchronous alert pattern.

Adaptive QoS prioritization logic may require feedback from Vehicle (e.g., actuator readiness) or Safety Services (e.g., safety state); if such feedback is frequent or blocking, diffusion increases. Recommend decoupling via telemetry polling rather than synchronous callbacks.

If multiple teleoperation modes (e.g., direct control, supervised autonomy) have different latency requirements, the DP may need refinement to parameterize QoS policies per mode; current DP assumes single p95 ≤ 150 ms budget.
assigned_im.recommended_revision: DP is well-scoped and containable as stated. No revision needed. However, recommend clarifying the DP to explicitly state: (1) QoS prioritization is adaptive based on network conditions and operator intent (not static); (2) latency monitoring includes both command-to-actuator and sensor-to-operator paths; (3) SafetyAlert is triggered on p95 breach, not on every latency spike. This refinement improves testability and reduces ambiguity about what 'adaptive' means in the context of the Teleoperation module.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: High. The p95 ≤ 150 ms acceptance criterion is measurable and testable within the Teleoperation module via instrumented latency monitoring on both command-to-actuator and sensor-to-operator paths. End-to-end latency can be verified under agreed network conditions (e.g., simulated or live network profiles). SafetyAlert escalation provides a clear signal for SLA breach detection.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Clear. The Teleoperation module owns the deterministic communication pipeline and latency SLA enforcement. Ownership boundaries are well-defined: Teleoperation handles pipeline logic and monitoring; Safety Services consumes alerts and enforces safety policy; Vehicle executes actuator commands. The FR-to-IM mapping is direct and unambiguous.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP1 directly addresses the FR requirement. The deterministic pipeline, latency monitoring, and adaptive QoS prioritization are necessary and sufficient mechanisms to enable safe remote operation within the p95 ≤ 150 ms budget. The DP is solution-neutral (does not prescribe specific protocols or hardware) and logically sound. No logical gaps or contradictions detected.

diffusion_risks: Network Link abstraction opacity: If the Network Link interface does not expose sufficient detail (e.g., packet timestamps, queue depths, link utilization), Teleoperation may require intrusive coupling to measure end-to-end latency accurately. Recommend explicit latency telemetry from Network Link (e.g., per-packet round-trip time, jitter estimates).

SafetyAlert synchronous blocking: If Safety Services responds synchronously to SafetyAlert, the response path could violate the p95 ≤ 150 ms budget. Recommend asynchronous, fire-and-forget alert pattern with optional acknowledgment outside the critical path.

Adaptive QoS feedback loops: If adaptive QoS prioritization requires frequent or blocking feedback from Vehicle (e.g., actuator load, readiness state) or Safety Services (e.g., safety mode transitions), diffusion increases and latency predictability degrades. Recommend decoupling via periodic telemetry polling (e.g., 10 Hz) rather than synchronous callbacks.

Multi-mode latency requirements: If the NGHE solution supports multiple teleoperation modes (e.g., direct control, supervised autonomy, failsafe) with different latency budgets, the current DP assumes a single p95 ≤ 150 ms threshold. Recommend parameterizing QoS policies per mode to avoid diffusion of mode-specific logic across Teleoperation and other modules.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: Excellent containment (0.92 score). The Teleoperation module is the natural owner of the deterministic pipeline, latency monitoring, and adaptive QoS prioritization. Cohesion is high: all three mechanisms are core teleoperation concerns. Diffusion is minimal (0.08): SafetyAlert is a clean outbound interface; Network Link abstraction isolates transport details; Tasking and Telemetry interfaces align with operator command and sensor feedback flows. Ownership is unambiguous—Teleoperation owns the end-to-end latency SLA. No competing modules (Vehicle, Safety Services, NGHE Solution) are better candidates. Recommend documenting the Network Link abstraction contract explicitly to prevent latency measurement coupling.

recommended_revisions: Clarify DP1 to explicitly state that adaptive QoS prioritization is dynamic and responsive to network conditions (e.g., congestion, packet loss, jitter) and operator intent (e.g., safety-critical vs. non-critical commands). Define the adaptation policy (e.g., reduce sensor feedback resolution under congestion, prioritize safety commands over telemetry).

Refine DP1 to specify that latency monitoring includes both command-to-actuator (ingress-to-egress) and sensor-to-operator (egress-to-ingress) paths. Clarify whether p95 ≤ 150 ms applies to each path independently or to the round-trip aggregate.

Specify that SafetyAlert is triggered on sustained or repeated p95 breaches (e.g., 3 consecutive measurements exceed 150 ms), not on every transient latency spike. This reduces alert noise and improves safety signal fidelity.

Document the Network Link abstraction contract: define what latency telemetry (e.g., per-packet timestamps, queue depths, link utilization) is exposed to Teleoperation. Ensure Teleoperation can measure end-to-end latency without intrusive coupling to transport-layer details.

Specify the SafetyAlert interface as asynchronous and non-blocking. Recommend a fire-and-forget pattern with optional acknowledgment outside the critical latency path to prevent Safety Services from blocking the teleoperation pipeline.

If multiple teleoperation modes are supported, parameterize the DP to allow mode-specific latency budgets and QoS policies. Document how mode transitions are coordinated between Teleoperation and Safety Services to avoid diffusion of mode logic.

Add acceptance criterion for latency monitoring accuracy: e.g., 'Latency measurement error ≤ 5 ms under agreed network conditions.' This ensures the p95 ≤ 150 ms SLA is verifiable and not undermined by measurement uncertainty.

decision.justification: All three gates (FR-IM, FR-DP, DP-IM) pass with high confidence. The FR is well-defined, the DP is logically sound and solution-neutral, and the IM (Teleoperation module) is the correct owner with excellent containment (0.92). However, the recommended revisions reveal critical design details that must be decomposed into sub-FRs to ensure the DP is fully realizable and the diffusion risks are mitigated. Specifically: (1) adaptive QoS prioritization requires explicit sub-requirements for network condition sensing, policy adaptation, and mode-specific parameterization; (2) latency monitoring requires sub-requirements for dual-path measurement (command-to-actuator and sensor-to-operator), accuracy bounds, and breach detection logic; (3) SafetyAlert interface requires sub-requirements for asynchronous, non-blocking signaling and alert threshold tuning. These sub-FRs are necessary to operationalize the DP and reduce diffusion risks (Network Link opacity, SafetyAlert blocking, adaptive feedback loops, multi-mode latency). Decomposition will also establish clear ownership boundaries for latency telemetry contracts and mode-specific QoS policies, improving architectural clarity and testability.
next_level_sub_frs: FR1.1: Measure end-to-end latency on both command-to-actuator and sensor-to-operator paths with accuracy ≤ 5 ms under agreed network conditions.
FR1.2: Detect sustained or repeated p95 latency breaches (e.g., 3 consecutive measurements exceed 150 ms threshold) and trigger SafetyAlert without blocking the teleoperation pipeline.
FR1.3: Sense network conditions (congestion, packet loss, jitter, link utilization) in real-time and expose latency telemetry via Network Link abstraction contract.
FR1.4: Adapt QoS prioritization dynamically in response to network conditions and operator intent (e.g., reduce sensor feedback resolution under congestion, prioritize safety-critical commands).
FR1.5: Support mode-specific latency budgets and QoS policies (e.g., direct control, supervised autonomy, failsafe) and coordinate mode transitions with Safety Services asynchronously.
FR1.6: Signal latency SLA breaches to Safety Services via asynchronous, fire-and-forget SafetyAlert interface with optional acknowledgment outside the critical latency path.

revise.why: The current DP1 is logically sound but underspecifies critical mechanisms required to operationalize the deterministic pipeline and mitigate diffusion risks. Revisions should: (1) explicitly state that adaptive QoS prioritization is dynamic and responsive to network conditions and operator intent, with a defined adaptation policy; (2) clarify that latency monitoring covers both command-to-actuator and sensor-to-operator paths independently, with p95 ≤ 150 ms applied per path or round-trip as appropriate; (3) specify that SafetyAlert is triggered on sustained breaches (e.g., 3 consecutive measurements), not transient spikes, to reduce alert noise; (4) document the Network Link abstraction contract to expose latency telemetry (per-packet timestamps, queue depths, link utilization) and prevent intrusive coupling; (5) specify SafetyAlert as asynchronous and non-blocking to prevent Safety Services from blocking the teleoperation pipeline; (6) parameterize the DP to support mode-specific latency budgets and QoS policies if multiple teleoperation modes are supported; (7) add acceptance criterion for latency monitoring accuracy (e.g., ≤ 5 ms measurement error). These revisions will make the DP fully realizable and reduce ambiguity in the IM layer.

assumptions: Network Link abstraction will expose sufficient latency telemetry (per-packet timestamps, queue depths, link utilization) to enable accurate end-to-end latency measurement without intrusive coupling to transport-layer details.
Safety Services will consume SafetyAlert asynchronously and non-blockingly, allowing Teleoperation to continue operating without waiting for acknowledgment.

Adaptive QoS prioritization can be implemented via periodic telemetry polling (e.g., 10 Hz) rather than synchronous callbacks to Vehicle or Safety Services, decoupling feedback loops and preserving latency predictability.

The p95 ≤ 150 ms latency budget is sufficient for safe remote operation under agreed network conditions (e.g., simulated or live network profiles with defined packet loss, jitter, and congestion characteristics).
Multiple teleoperation modes (if supported) have distinct latency requirements that can be parameterized independently without diffusing mode-specific logic across Teleoperation and other modules.
Latency measurement error can be bounded to ≤ 5 ms under agreed network conditions, ensuring the p95 ≤ 150 ms SLA is verifiable and not undermined by measurement uncertainty.
open_risks: Network Link abstraction opacity: If Network Link does not expose sufficient latency telemetry, Teleoperation may require intrusive coupling to transport-layer details, increasing diffusion and reducing latency predictability. Mitigation: Explicitly document Network Link abstraction contract and latency telemetry requirements during IM design.

SafetyAlert synchronous blocking: If Safety Services responds synchronously to SafetyAlert, the response path could violate the p95 ≤ 150 ms budget. Mitigation: Specify SafetyAlert as asynchronous, fire-and-forget pattern with optional acknowledgment outside the critical path.

Adaptive QoS feedback loops: If adaptive QoS prioritization requires frequent or blocking feedback from Vehicle or Safety Services, diffusion increases and latency predictability degrades. Mitigation: Decouple via periodic telemetry polling (e.g., 10 Hz) rather than synchronous callbacks.

Multi-mode latency requirements: If the NGHE solution supports multiple teleoperation modes with different latency budgets, the current DP assumes a single p95 ≤ 150 ms threshold. Mitigation: Parameterize QoS policies per mode to avoid diffusion of mode-specific logic.

Latency measurement accuracy: If latency monitoring error exceeds 5 ms, the p95 ≤ 150 ms SLA may be unverifiable or undermined by measurement uncertainty. Mitigation: Add acceptance criterion for latency monitoring accuracy and validate under agreed network conditions.

Alert threshold tuning: If SafetyAlert is triggered on every transient latency spike, alert noise may reduce safety signal fidelity and operator trust. Mitigation: Specify SafetyAlert trigger on sustained or repeated breaches (e.g., 3 consecutive measurements exceed 150 ms).
evidence_needed: Network Link abstraction contract specification: Define what latency telemetry (per-packet timestamps, queue depths, link utilization) is exposed to Teleoperation and how it is accessed without intrusive coupling.

SafetyAlert interface specification: Document the asynchronous, non-blocking signaling pattern, including message format, delivery guarantees, and optional acknowledgment mechanism outside the critical latency path.

Adaptive QoS prioritization policy: Define the adaptation logic (e.g., network condition thresholds, sensor feedback resolution reduction, command prioritization rules) and how it responds to operator intent and safety-critical signals.

Mode-specific latency budgets and QoS policies: If multiple teleoperation modes are supported, document the latency requirements and QoS policies for each mode and how mode transitions are coordinated with Safety Services.

Latency monitoring accuracy validation: Measure latency monitoring error under agreed network conditions (e.g., simulated or live network profiles with defined packet loss, jitter, and congestion) and verify that error ≤ 5 ms.

End-to-end latency measurement methodology: Clarify whether p95 ≤ 150 ms applies to command-to-actuator path, sensor-to-operator path, or round-trip aggregate, and document the measurement points and calculation method.

SafetyAlert breach detection logic: Specify the algorithm for detecting sustained or repeated p95 breaches (e.g., 3 consecutive measurements exceed 150 ms) and the time window for aggregating measurements.
Integration testing plan: Define test scenarios for latency monitoring, adaptive QoS prioritization, SafetyAlert signaling, and mode transitions under various network conditions and operator intents.
